patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 62-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start by saying: “I’ve been having bleeding from my bottom and this sore lump that won’t go away.” Early in the visit, volunteer: bright red blood on toilet paper/sometimes in the bowl, a tender anus lump that seems slightly bigger, pain with bowel movements, and itching/irritation. Mention you tried OTC hemorrhoid cream/wipes with only slight itch relief.

If asked about other bowel/rectal symptoms, share: feeling like you still need to go after finishing, mucus/wet discharge, stools thinner than usual, more fatigue, a few unintentional pounds lost, and occasional crampy lower belly discomfort.

Do not mention unless directly asked or trust is built: nighttime bathroom trips from pressure, dull deep pelvic ache when sitting, faint smell/spotting in underwear, brief flushed/warm episodes.

Be reluctant/embarrassed about: trouble holding gas, rare stool leakage, and painful sex—only disclose if asked sensitively.

Express misconceptions: you think it’s hemorrhoids or a tear from constipation. If clinician shows empathy, thank them, relax, and answer more fully. Ask about costs/insurance and time off work; assume newer treatments are better but likely unaffordable.

diagnosis.primary: neuroendocrine tumor of the anal canal

correct_diagnosis.primary: Neuroendocrine tumor (NET) of the anal canal
acceptable_alternatives: High-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma (small cell/large cell) of the anal canal
Anal canal carcinoma with neuroendocrine differentiation
required_evidence: Histopathology from anal canal lesion biopsy confirming neuroendocrine morphology
Immunohistochemistry supportive of NET (e.g., synaptophysin and/or chromogranin positive)
Tumor grade/proliferation index reported (Ki-67 and/or mitotic rate) to distinguish well-differentiated NET vs high-grade NEC
Anorectal mass identified on physical exam/anoscopy/proctoscopy with documentation of size and location
Staging evaluation planned or initiated (cross-sectional imaging; consider somatostatin receptor imaging for well-differentiated NET)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low (consistent with anemia, often iron-deficiency if chronic bleeding)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Low (microcytosis) if iron-deficiency; may be normal early
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to high (reactive thrombocytosis possible with iron-deficiency/inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal (may be mildly elevated with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Low (iron-deficiency); can be normal/high if significant inflammation—interpret with clinical context

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to colorectal surgery and oncology for definitive management and staging
Biopsy confirmation and grading with Ki-67/mitotic index if not already obtained
Staging workup: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast; consider pelvic MRI for local staging; consider somatostatin receptor PET (e.g., Ga-68 DOTATATE) if well-differentiated NET suspected
If high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma: plan for systemic chemotherapy (platinum + etoposide) and discuss combined modality therapy with oncology/radiation oncology

If well-differentiated localized NET: discuss surgical resection approach based on size, depth, margins, nodal status; consider somatostatin analog therapy for functional or metastatic disease per oncology
Manage suspected/confirmed bleeding: counsel on monitoring for worsening bleeding, dizziness, syncope; consider transfusion/IV iron based on severity and symptoms (coordinate with treating team)
Nutrition counseling to increase dietary iron and optimize absorption (take iron away from calcium/antacids; with vitamin C if tolerated)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for review of pathology/staging plan and reassessment of anemia symptoms; earlier (24-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia or ongoing significant bleeding

must_include: Diagnosis is a neuroendocrine tumor arising in the anal canal and requires biopsy confirmation and grading
Grade (Ki-67/mitotic rate) and stage determine treatment and prognosis
Anemia/iron deficiency can occur from chronic tumor bleeding and is treatable while definitive cancer therapy is arranged
Need for specialist-led staging and treatment planning (colorectal surgery + oncology; possibly radiation oncology)
Supportive treatment for iron-deficiency anemia with oral iron and monitoring response
Potential definitive therapies vary: surgery for localized well-differentiated disease vs chemotherapy (often platinum/etoposide) and radiation for high-grade disease
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: heavy rectal bleeding, fainting, chest pain, shortness of breath, severe weakness
Discuss expected side effects of iron (GI upset, constipation, dark stools) and strategies to improve tolerance
Confirm and document allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and clarify reaction severity
How to take iron for best absorption (every other day dosing, avoid taking with calcium/antacids, consider vitamin C)
Dietary iron sources and hydration/fiber to reduce constipation
Importance of attending staging and oncology follow-up promptly

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: rectal bleeding, bowel changes, pain, weight loss, flushing/diarrhea, duration, severity; review anemia symptoms; current meds/supplements (esp. antacids/calcium), pregnancy status if relevant; past cancer workup/biopsy status; comorbidities affecting iron or therapy.

Assess anemia and iron deficiency and establish baseline prior to supportive treatment: CBC with indices, ferritin, iron/TIBC/transferrin saturation; consider CMP for baseline (renal/hepatic) if prescribing/anticipating oncology therapy.
Confirm iron-deficiency anemia pattern and degree of anemia to guide urgency, dosing, and follow-up timeline.
State working diagnosis and what is required to confirm: anal canal neuroendocrine tumor requires biopsy confirmation with grading (Ki-67/mitotic rate) and staging; link anemia to chronic bleeding.

Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm and document allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and reaction severity; note that oral iron is generally unrelated but must still be verified per protocol.

Noncritical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions affecting iron absorption (calcium, antacids/PPIs, levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones, bisphosphonates) and adjust administration timing.

Start supportive therapy for iron-deficiency anemia with an oral iron regimen (e.g., ferrous sulfate providing ~40–65 mg elemental iron every other day) plus bowel regimen advice as needed; include monitoring plan.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (biopsy, grade/stage), treatment pathways (specialist-led, surgery vs chemo/radiation depending on grade), anemia management, red flags, iron side effects and tolerance strategies, and lifestyle/administration guidance for iron.

Arrange prompt referrals and follow-up: colorectal surgery + medical oncology (± radiation oncology) for staging/treatment planning; recheck labs in ~2–4 weeks (or per severity) to confirm hemoglobin response; ensure urgent evaluation plan for red flags.
Close after confirming understanding, providing return precautions, and documenting plan.

diagnosis.primary: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS), glycosylation pathway defect
required_evidence: History of fatigable weakness with onset in infancy/childhood and fluctuating course (ocular/bulbar/limb ± respiratory involvement)
Clinical pattern consistent with neuromuscular junction transmission defect (worsens with exertion/illness; improves with rest)
Electrodiagnostic evidence of neuromuscular transmission defect (decrement on low-frequency RNS and/or increased jitter on SFEMG)
Genetic confirmation of a glycosylation-pathway CMS gene (e.g., GMPPB, DPAGT1, ALG2/ALG14, GFPT1) OR strong prior documentation of such diagnosis
Exclusion of autoimmune myasthenia gravis when relevant (negative AChR/MuSK/LRP4 antibodies and/or non-supportive clinical context)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (may be elevated only with intercurrent infection)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Usually normal; may be elevated if chronic hypoventilation/CO2 retention is present

medications: Pyridostigmine 30-60 mg PO q4-6h while awake (titrate to benefit and tolerability; consider ER formulation at bedtime if nocturnal symptoms)
3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 10 mg PO TID, titrate by 5-10 mg/dose every 3-4 days to effect; max 80 mg/day (specialist-directed)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO TID (or 4 mg PO BID) as adjunct in selected glycosylation-defect CMS (specialist-directed)
non_pharmacological: Neuromuscular specialist follow-up and confirm genotype/phenotype-directed therapy; avoid empiric immunosuppression unless autoimmune MG is proven
Respiratory assessment for hypoventilation (symptom screen; consider spirometry/FVC and sleep evaluation if nocturnal hypoventilation suspected)
Swallow evaluation if bulbar symptoms/aspiration risk; nutrition optimization
Physical therapy with energy conservation strategies; avoid overexertion and treat intercurrent infections early
Medication avoidance plan for agents that worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, certain beta-blockers)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 2-4 weeks after medication initiation/titration, sooner (24-72 hours) if worsening bulbar/respiratory symptoms; ongoing specialist follow-up every 3-6 months

must_include: Condition is genetic and affects neuromuscular junction signaling (impaired transmission), not autoimmune
Symptoms fluctuate and can worsen with illness, certain medications, or fatigue; respiratory/bulbar symptoms are important warning signs
Why glycosylation-pathway defects can respond to specific symptomatic therapies (e.g., pyridostigmine, amifampridine, beta-agonists) and that responses vary by gene
Medication goals are symptom control and improved strength/endurance; not a cure
How to take pyridostigmine (timing with activities/meals) and common adverse effects (abdominal cramping, diarrhea, increased secretions, bradycardia) and when to seek care
Amifampridine risks (seizure risk, QT considerations) and need for specialist titration/monitoring
Beta-agonist adjunct (albuterol) potential side effects (tremor, palpitations) and monitoring
Explicitly document and respect allergies/intolerances: penicillin (urticaria) and azithromycin causing worsened weakness/dyspnea (neuromuscular exacerbation)
Provide an avoidance list for antibiotics/medications that can worsen weakness; discuss safer alternatives if infection occurs
Red-flag instructions: new/worsening dyspnea, inability to swallow, weak cough, cyanosis, rapidly progressive weakness → urgent evaluation/emergency care
Energy conservation and pacing; plan activities around peak medication effect
Infection prevention and prompt treatment; discuss vaccines as appropriate
Carry a medical alert/medication warning card noting CMS and drug sensitivities (macrolides including azithromycin)

Exclude alternative causes of recurrent infection/diarrhea: no supporting evidence for immunodeficiency/SCID (no severe recurrent opportunistic infections, no profound lymphopenia) and no evidence for invasive amoebiasis (no dysentery, liver abscess findings, or positive stool/serology)

non_pharmacological: Refer to thoracic surgery for definitive resection when feasible (e.g., sleeve resection/segmentectomy/lobectomy with lymph node sampling based on location and stage)

Avoid known carcinoid-syndrome triggers if symptomatic (alcohol, large meals, stress, serotonergic supplements); maintain hydration and monitor weight

must_include: A bronchial neuroendocrine (carcinoid) tumor is a type of lung tumor that can grow in or near airways and may cause cough, wheeze, recurrent pneumonia, or bleeding
Some tumors secrete hormones causing carcinoid syndrome (flushing and diarrhea), but not all do
Diagnosis and subtype/grading require tissue biopsy and imaging-based staging
Surgery is the preferred curative treatment when the tumor is localized and the patient is an operative candidate
If symptoms of hormone secretion occur or disease is unresectable/metastatic, somatostatin analog therapy (octreotide) can control symptoms and may slow growth
Need for staging tests and specialist referrals (thoracic surgery, pulmonology, oncology) before final treatment decisions
Discuss expected prognosis varies by typical vs atypical carcinoid and stage, and is often favorable when localized and resected
Review red flags requiring urgent care (significant hemoptysis, worsening shortness of breath, fever with suspected pneumonia, severe diarrhea/dehydration)
Acknowledge medication allergies (penicillin, codeine) and plan alternatives for pain control/antibiotics if needed
Smoking cessation support if applicable and avoidance of airway irritants

Importance of follow-up for surveillance imaging and symptom monitoring

Review returned results and integrate with clinical picture to support suspected bronchial neuroendocrine tumor and assess urgency (infection, anemia, organ dysfunction).
State working diagnosis of bronchial neuroendocrine (carcinoid) tumor and explicitly note that definitive diagnosis, grading (typical vs atypical), and staging require tissue biopsy and imaging.

Complete communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment options (surgery if localized; somatostatin analogs like octreotide for hormone symptoms/unresectable/metastatic), address prognosis and red flags, and provide lifestyle/trigger counseling and follow-up importance.
Before any prescribing, verify allergies (including reaction type/severity) and ensure alternatives for penicillin/codeine are planned.

Before prescribing, check for drug-drug interactions with current medications (including QT-prolonging agents, serotonergic drugs, anticoagulants as applicable) for any planned therapies (e.g., octreotide, antibiotics, analgesics).

If indicated, prescribe symptom-directed therapy consistent with safety checks (e.g., non-codeine analgesia; antibiotics not in penicillin class if pneumonia suspected; consider octreotide only in appropriate context and typically after specialist input).

Before ordering higher-risk tests/therapies, consider contraindications (e.g., contrast allergy/renal dysfunction for contrast imaging; pregnancy considerations). This check is non-critical but should be addressed when relevant.
Arrange timely referrals and follow-up: pulmonology for bronchoscopy/biopsy, thoracic surgery for resectability, oncology for staging/systemic options; plan surveillance and symptom monitoring.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting red flags, and ensuring follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounting clerk. Start with: “I keep getting more unsteady on my feet and I’m scared I’m going to fall.” Early in the visit, volunteer: walking “like I’m drunk,” more tripping/near-falls, clumsy hands (buttons/dropping things), occasional slurred speech when tired, slowly worsening over ~1 year.

If asked about details, add: shaky when reaching/using utensils, worse in the dark/uneven ground, brief dizziness when turning, eyes “jump” and reading is hard, occasional coughing with liquids, needs handrail on stairs, father had a balance disorder.

Do not volunteer at first: urinary urgency, fatigue after short walks, messy/larger handwriting, irritability/low mood, nighttime foot numbness.

Only if specifically asked, reluctantly admit: occasional urine accidents; partner says you cough/choke in sleep; you avoid social events because people think you’re intoxicated.

Express misconceptions: maybe inner ear, B12 deficiency, anxiety med side effect, or intermittent stroke.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Spinocerebellar ataxia (hereditary cerebellar ataxia)
acceptable_alternatives: Autosomal dominant spinocerebellar ataxia (SCA, unspecified type)
required_evidence: Progressive gait and limb ataxia with cerebellar signs (dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia)
Cerebellar dysarthria and/or nystagmus/ocular dysmetria
Chronic progressive course (months to years) rather than acute onset
Family history consistent with inherited ataxia OR onset age compatible with hereditary ataxia
Exclusion of common reversible mimics (medication/toxin effect, metabolic derangements, structural lesion) based on history, exam, and basic labs

Occupational therapy for adaptive devices and home safety evaluation (grab bars, remove trip hazards, assistive walking device as needed)
Speech-language pathology for dysarthria and swallow evaluation if choking/coughing with meals or weight loss; aspiration precautions as needed
Assess fall risk and prescribe assistive devices (cane/walker) and consider hip protectors if high risk
Avoid alcohol intoxication and sedating medications that worsen ataxia; review all current meds for contributors (benzodiazepines, anticonvulsants, antihistamines, opioids)
Driving safety counseling; consider formal driving assessment if impaired coordination/vision
Screen for depression/anxiety and caregiver burden; offer resources/support groups
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 4-8 weeks; earlier (1-2 weeks) if rapid progression, frequent falls, or dysphagia symptoms

diagnosis.primary: autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE), also termed sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy (familial form)
required_evidence: Recurrent stereotyped brief nocturnal events arising from sleep with hypermotor behaviors (e.g., thrashing, dystonic posturing, complex motor activity), often multiple per night
Abrupt onset/offset, short duration (typically seconds to <2 minutes), with post-event rapid recovery and minimal postictal confusion
Events cluster during NREM sleep and may be triggered by sleep deprivation
Personal or family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance (affected first-degree relatives) OR known pathogenic variant (e.g., CHRNA4/CHRNB2/CHRNA2/KCNT1 depending on family)
Exclusion of key mimics by history and/or targeted testing: parasomnias, psychogenic nonepileptic events, nocturnal panic, REM behavior disorder, obstructive sleep apnea-related arousals
Supportive testing when available: video-EEG polysomnography capturing events (ictal scalp EEG may be normal or subtle) and/or frontal seizure semiology on EEG

expected_lab_results.sodium: Typically normal at baseline; must be checked prior to and during therapy with carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine due to hyponatremia risk
expected_lab_results.creatinine: Typically normal; assess renal function for dosing if using renally cleared agents

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 100-200 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to 200-400 mg PO at bedtime (typical effective range 200-600 mg nightly; individualized)
If carbamazepine not tolerated/contraindicated: Oxcarbazepine 150 mg PO at bedtime; titrate to 300-600 mg PO at bedtime (monitor sodium closely)

If inadequate response or contraindications to sodium-channel agents: Lamotrigine 25 mg PO daily x 2 weeks, then 50 mg PO daily x 2 weeks, then increase by 50 mg/day every 1-2 weeks to 100-200 mg/day in divided doses (slow titration to reduce rash risk)
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis with overnight video-EEG polysomnography if not already captured; consider extended ambulatory EEG when PSG unavailable
Screen for and treat comorbid sleep disorders (especially obstructive sleep apnea) that can worsen nocturnal seizures
Sleep hygiene and seizure trigger reduction: consistent sleep schedule, avoid sleep deprivation, limit alcohol, avoid recreational drugs
Safety counseling for nocturnal seizures: bed safety (remove sharp objects, consider low bed), supervise bathing/swimming, driving restrictions per local regulations until seizure control achieved
Genetic counseling and family screening discussion if familial pattern suspected/confirmed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 4-6 weeks for titration and response; sooner (1-2 weeks) if adverse effects. Repeat sodium and basic metabolic panel 1-2 weeks after starting/titrating carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine and periodically thereafter.

must_include: Events are most consistent with sleep-related focal seizures from the frontal lobe (often brief, stereotyped, hypermotor, clustered at night)
This condition can run in families (autosomal dominant pattern) and genetic counseling/testing may be considered
Key differentials (parasomnias, panic, REM behavior disorder, OSA arousals) and why seizures are favored based on stereotypy, brevity, clustering, and abrupt onset/offset
Need for confirmatory testing with video-EEG polysomnography when feasible; normal scalp EEG does not exclude frontal lobe seizures
First-line therapy commonly uses carbamazepine (or oxcarbazepine) taken at night; goal is to reduce/stop nocturnal events
Titration plan and expected timeframe to see improvement
Common side effects (dizziness, drowsiness, nausea) and serious symptoms requiring urgent evaluation (rash, facial swelling, fever, severe fatigue/confusion)
Need for lab monitoring (especially sodium for carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine) and follow-up for dose adjustment
Teratogenic risk counseling and contraception planning if of childbearing potential; discuss folic acid supplementation if applicable
Discuss impact on driving/work safety and local legal requirements for seizure reporting/driving restrictions
Review medication interactions (carbamazepine is a strong enzyme inducer and can reduce effectiveness of hormonal contraception and interact with many drugs)
Clarify penicillin allergy is not directly related to antiseizure medication choice but will be documented and checked for all prescriptions
Shared decision-making on medication choice considering comorbidities, pregnancy plans, and tolerability
Avoid sleep deprivation and maintain regular sleep schedule to reduce seizure risk
Limit alcohol and avoid recreational drugs that can lower seizure threshold
Nocturnal safety measures (safe sleep environment; avoid sleeping in high-risk settings)
When to seek urgent care (prolonged seizure >5 minutes, repeated seizures without recovery, serious injury, new neurologic deficits, severe medication reaction)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit event semiology and timing to distinguish sleep-related focal seizures from parasomnias/panic/RBD/OSA arousals; assess frequency/cluster pattern, stereotypy, abrupt onset/offset, duration, awareness, injuries, incontinence, tongue biting, post-ictal confusion, triggers (sleep deprivation/alcohol), and response to prior meds.
Assess red flags and safety: status-like events, daytime seizures, new focal deficits, serious injuries, SUDEP risk factors; review driving/work hazards and local restrictions.

Collect past medical history and comorbidities relevant to antiseizure meds (hepatic disease, hyponatremia history, cardiac conduction disease, psychiatric history), current medication list (including hormonal contraception), substance use, and pregnancy potential/plan.
Critical pre-prescribing safety check: confirm and document allergies (including penicillin history and any prior antiseizure drug rashes) before selecting carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine.

Critical pre-prescribing safety check: evaluate for interactions (notably carbamazepine enzyme induction reducing hormonal contraception efficacy; interactions with warfarin, DOACs, macrolides, azoles, antiretrovirals, psychotropics, grapefruit, etc.).
If ordering diagnostic sedation or additional meds, ensure no interactions with current regimen (non-critical ordering-related check is acceptable but should be considered).

Critical pre-prescribing safety check: screen for contraindications to carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine (history of serious cutaneous adverse reactions, significant hepatic disease, bone marrow suppression, hyponatremia risk, cardiac conduction issues; pregnancy considerations and alternative options if needed).
Non-critical pre-ordering check: ensure no contraindications to planned testing (e.g., PSG/vEEG feasibility, safety for sleep lab, ability to withhold meds if planned).

Order confirmatory testing: video-EEG polysomnography when feasible; consider additional baseline labs prior to carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine (CBC, CMP including sodium and liver enzymes) to support safe initiation and monitoring.
Review available results (if any) and explicitly note that normal scalp EEG does not exclude nocturnal frontal lobe seizures; integrate with clinical features.
Make working diagnosis: sleep-related focal seizures consistent with autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy; document differential and rationale.

Explain diagnosis with required elements: frontal lobe sleep-related focal seizures; familial autosomal dominant pattern and genetic counseling/testing; differentials and why seizures favored; need for vEEG-PSG and limitations of scalp EEG.

Initiate first-line therapy (commonly carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine, often dosed at night), with titration plan, timeframe for improvement, side effects/serious reactions, and lab monitoring plan (especially sodium). Ensure all critical safety checks completed before this step.

Address concerns: driving/work safety and legal requirements; interactions (enzyme induction and contraception); clarify penicillin allergy relevance; shared decision-making incorporating comorbidities, pregnancy plans, and tolerability.

Lifestyle and safety counseling: sleep hygiene, avoid sleep deprivation/alcohol/recreational drugs, nocturnal safety measures, and urgent care triggers (seizure >5 min, clusters without recovery, injury, new deficits, severe med reaction).
Arrange follow-up to review test results, assess response/tolerability, adjust dose, and repeat labs (sodium and other monitoring as indicated); discuss genetic counseling referral if appropriate.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 46-year-old male IT systems analyst. You are cooperative and speak clearly, using practical, detail-oriented language. Start by volunteering: your feet have been getting weaker and you trip a lot, especially on stairs; your toes catch the ground; your hands are weaker (opening jars/gripping tools); and your lower legs look skinnier. If asked, explain it’s been slowly worsening over 2 years, with faster fatigue on long walks, occasional nighttime calf cramps, mild trouble swallowing pills, shoulder weakness overhead, and hip/thigh aching after activity (more weakness than pain). Do not volunteer vivid dreams/acting out, forgetfulness/word-finding issues, needing hands to rise from a low chair, shortness of breath when lying flat (use extra pillows), or voice hoarseness unless specifically asked about sleep, cognition, breathing, or speech. Be reluctant/embarrassed about erectile problems and occasional urge incontinence; only disclose if asked directly and respond briefly. Mention misconceptions: you suspected a pinched nerve due to low back aches, or shoes/flat feet; magnesium didn’t help. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become slightly more open. Raise cost/insurance worries about genetic testing and PT limits.

Mild-to-moderate CK elevation (often normal to <10x ULN) consistent with VCP myopathy
Exclusion of common mimics with available labs (normal thyroid function and B12; no diabetic neuropathy explanation)
History and exam not consistent with primary inflammatory myopathy (e.g., no prominent myalgias/systemic features; CK not markedly high)

Start by saying: “I keep feeling like people at school are watching me and talking about me.” Also volunteer you’re on edge around others, avoiding friends, grades dropping/can’t focus, and sleeping poorly because your mind won’t shut off. If asked about mood/anxiety, mention racing heart and shakiness and wonder if it’s anxiety/panic, ADHD, or depression. Only if directly asked about hallucinations, paranoia, hygiene, or thought problems, admit you sometimes hear a mean voice when alone, feel online messages have hidden hints for you, shower less/don’t care about clothes, and your thoughts get jumbled. Do not volunteer hidden behaviors unless specifically probed (patterns in numbers/posts, curtains closed/avoid windows, missed meals/weight loss, forgetfulness). Be hesitant but honest about weed making it worse, occasional thoughts of hurting yourself “to make my brain stop” without a plan, and feeling like thoughts are “put in my head” only if asked about substances, safety, or psychotic symptoms. If shown empathy, you relax slightly, say you’re scared/embarrassed, and ask about low-cost care, missing work, and records affecting scholarships/jobs.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 56-year-old anxious but clear-speaking woman. Open with: “I’ve had this cough and I get winded way easier than I used to.” Volunteer: dry cough for 5–6 weeks, SOB with stairs/groceries, fatigue, low-grade evening fevers, antibiotics for “bronchitis” didn’t help. If asked, add: tight/sore chest with deep breaths, occasional chills/night sweats, mild unintentional weight loss/low appetite, mostly dry cough with small clear/white mucus, no wheeze, worse in cold air, no hemoptysis. Do not mention unless specifically asked: puffy fingertips/rounded nails, home pulse ox drops to high 80s with walking, can’t lie flat without coughing, morning hand stiffness 30–60 min. Withhold and only admit if asked directly about substances/meds: THC vaping a few times weekly; taking leftover prednisone in short bursts because it helps. Express worries/misconceptions: fear of pulmonary embolism from calf soreness after car ride; racing heart could be anxiety/heart; reflux causing cough; possible rheumatoid arthritis; fear of sudden “lung scarring”/lung failure. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and become more willing to disclose. Push cost/work concerns: want to space out CT/PFTs, prefer telehealth, ask for inhalers instead of oral steroids to save money.

medications: Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg 2 puffs inhaled q4-6h PRN wheeze/SOB
confounders: Recent presumed community-acquired pneumonia treated with azithromycin with minimal improvement
Chronic GERD with possible microaspiration contributing to cough and basilar opacities
Remote exposure to pet birds (raises concern for hypersensitivity pneumonitis overlap)
Former smoker (15 pack-years; quit 8 years ago) complicating interpretation of chronic respiratory symptoms

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP)
acceptable_alternatives: Secondary organizing pneumonia (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis–associated organizing pneumonia)
Acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP) (early presentation; requires exclusion)
Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) (if dyspnea predominant; requires exclusion)
required_evidence: Subacute (weeks) progressive dyspnea and nonproductive cough with systemic symptoms (fatigue, low-grade fever) and poor/partial response to empiric antibiotics
Inflammatory markers elevated (CRP and/or ESR) with low/normal procalcitonin supporting non-bacterial inflammation
CBC without marked neutrophilic leukocytosis

Clinical exclusion of alternative causes: no clear infectious source, no overt heart failure, no clear provoking thromboembolism history; consider autoimmune features (RA symptoms) and thromboembolic risk assessment
Imaging consistent with organizing pneumonia pattern (e.g., patchy peripheral consolidations/ground-glass opacities on CXR/CT) and/or bronchoscopy/BAL negative for infection when performed

expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (often <12 x10^9/L); no marked neutrophilic leukocytosis
expected_lab_results.CBC Hemoglobin: Normal or mild anemia of inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Low or normal (argues against bacterial pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.CMP Sodium: Usually normal; may be mildly low in systemic illness
expected_lab_results.CMP Potassium: Usually normal (baseline important prior to corticosteroids/diuretics if used)

medications: Prednisone 0.75 mg/kg/day PO daily for 2-4 weeks, then taper gradually over 3-6 months based on clinical and radiographic response

Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 1 double-strength tablet (160/800 mg) PO three times weekly for Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis if prednisone-equivalent >=20 mg/day expected for >=4 weeks and no contraindications
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (GI protection if high-dose steroids and risk factors present)
non_pharmacological: Assess oxygenation; provide supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >= 92% (or individualized target if chronic hypercapnia present)
Pulmonology follow-up; consider HRCT and pulmonary function testing (spirometry + DLCO) to document baseline and response
If diagnosis uncertain or poor response to steroids: bronchoscopy with BAL to exclude infection; consider lung biopsy per specialist
Evaluate for secondary causes (including rheumatoid arthritis): history, exam, and rheumatology referral if suggestive
Vaccination review (influenza, pneumococcal) before/early in steroid course when feasible
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for symptom and oxygenation response; repeat imaging and/or PFTs in 4-6 weeks; earlier review if worsening dyspnea, fever, or hypoxemia

must_include: COP is an inflammatory lung condition (not necessarily infection) where small airways/alveoli fill with organizing inflammatory tissue
Reason for diagnosis: subacute symptoms plus elevated CRP/ESR with low procalcitonin and lack of strong bacterial features; imaging pattern supports
Need to rule out secondary causes (autoimmune disease like rheumatoid arthritis, drug/toxin exposure) and important alternatives (blood clots/CTEPH, acute interstitial pneumonia)
Main treatment is corticosteroids with expected improvement over days to weeks; taper is slow to prevent relapse
Discuss common steroid adverse effects (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, weight gain, hypertension, infection risk, bone loss) and monitoring plan
If prolonged high-dose steroids: rationale for Pneumocystis prophylaxis and when it is used
Antibiotics are not routinely needed unless evidence of bacterial infection emerges
Red flags requiring urgent care: worsening shortness of breath, new chest pain, hemoptysis, high fever, syncope, or low oxygen readings
Relapse possibility and importance of follow-up imaging/PFTs
Clarify penicillin allergy and ensure it is documented; avoid beta-lactams if immediate-type allergy unless specialist evaluation
Infection prevention while on steroids (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts, prompt evaluation for fever)
Bone protection strategies during steroid therapy (adequate calcium/vitamin D intake, weight-bearing exercise as tolerated; consider bone health assessment)
Smoking cessation and pulmonary rehabilitation/activity pacing if dyspneic

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for COP and alternatives: duration (subacute), cough/dyspnea, fever, pleuritic chest pain/hemoptysis, oxygen needs, exposures (drugs/toxins), autoimmune symptoms, smoking, VTE risks, prior antibiotics response, pregnancy status if relevant.

Order labs to support inflammatory pattern and exclude infection/secondary causes: CBC with diff, CMP, CRP/ESR, procalcitonin, respiratory viral testing as appropriate; consider autoimmune screen (ANA, RF/anti-CCP) if symptoms; baseline glucose/A1c if starting steroids.
Interpret results (elevated CRP/ESR with low procalcitonin; no strong bacterial leukocytosis) to support COP vs bacterial pneumonia and guide whether antibiotics are indicated.

Make working diagnosis of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia based on subacute presentation plus supportive labs and imaging pattern; explicitly note need to rule out secondary organizing pneumonia and key alternatives (PE/CTEPH, acute interstitial pneumonia).

Explain diagnosis in patient-centered language, including that COP is inflammatory (not necessarily infection), why it fits current findings, and what additional evaluation may be needed to exclude secondary causes and important alternatives.
Critical safety check before any prescribing: confirm and document allergies (including clarifying penicillin reaction type/timing) and implications for future antibiotics.

Critical safety check before prescribing: assess interactions with current meds (e.g., anticoagulants, diabetes meds, NSAIDs, immunosuppressants) relevant to corticosteroids and any prophylactic agents.

Initiate corticosteroid therapy with a plan for monitoring and slow taper; consider Pneumocystis prophylaxis if prolonged high-dose steroids are planned; avoid routine antibiotics unless evidence for bacterial infection.

Explain treatment expectations (improvement days–weeks), taper rationale, steroid adverse effects and monitoring (BP, glucose, mood/sleep, weight, infection risk, bone health), and when Pneumocystis prophylaxis is used; emphasize antibiotics not routine without bacterial evidence.

Address concerns: red flags needing urgent care (worsening dyspnea, new chest pain, hemoptysis, high fever, syncope, low O2); relapse risk and follow-up imaging/PFTs; reinforce allergy documentation and beta-lactam avoidance if immediate-type allergy unless evaluated.

Lifestyle and prevention counseling: infection precautions while on steroids, bone protection (calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise as tolerated, consider bone health assessment), smoking cessation, pulmonary rehab/activity pacing.
Arrange close follow-up to assess response, monitor steroid side effects, and plan repeat imaging/PFTs; ensure escalation plan if deterioration.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and ensuring follow-up and safety-net instructions.

If asked about urinary changes, share: peeing less despite similar fluids, occasional foamy urine. If asked about pain or other symptoms, mention dull low back/side ache, headaches, 5-lb weight gain this week, and a bad sore throat about 2 weeks ago that resolved. Do NOT volunteer unless asked directly: higher BP at a pharmacy machine, mild shortness of breath on stairs, frequent ibuprofen use for a sports injury, and high-protein shakes. Be reluctant about party drugs: if asked about substances, admit ecstasy last weekend with little water and worry it caused harm. Offer a misleading concern: intermittent fever/chills/body aches with occasional belly pain/loose stools; wonder about a rare metabolism issue you read online. Express cost/time worries: question repeated urine/blood tests, fear missing work, and believe generics are lower quality. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more open.

medications: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03 mg/0.15 mg 1 tablet daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 8 hours as needed for pain (self-directed)
confounders: Recent NSAID use (can worsen kidney function and cause interstitial nephritis)
Recent upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago (can suggest post-infectious or IgA-related nephritis)
Combined oral contraceptive use (can contribute to elevated blood pressure and edema)
Menstrual contamination risk for hematuria on urinalysis if sample not clean-catch

correct_diagnosis.primary: Nephritis (glomerulonephritis causing acute kidney injury)
required_evidence: Kidney function impairment on available labs (elevated serum creatinine and/or BUN with reduced eGFR)
Electrolyte/acid-base pattern compatible with reduced renal function (e.g., hyperkalemia and/or low bicarbonate)
Clinical nephritic features should be elicited despite not being provided (hematuria/cola-colored urine, edema, hypertension, reduced urine output) and used to support the diagnosis

Differential mevalonic aciduria is less consistent with isolated renal dysfunction and typically presents with recurrent inflammatory episodes/fever and systemic features; absence of those features supports nephritis over mevalonic aciduria

expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated; may be disproportionate depending on volume status
expected_lab_results.eGFR (CKD-EPI): Reduced (may be <60 mL/min/1.73 m2 in moderate AKI/renal impairment)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal to mildly low (dilutional hyponatremia possible with fluid retention)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to elevated (hyperkalemia risk with reduced GFR)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Usually normal; may vary with acid-base status
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low if metabolic acidosis from reduced renal acid excretion
expected_lab_results.Calcium: Usually normal in acute nephritis; may be low if significant renal dysfunction

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO/IV once, then titrate (e.g., 20-80 mg) to achieve euvolemia if volume overloaded
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily for hypertension (titrate to BP goal) if needed
Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate 10 g PO three times daily for up to 48 hours if hyperkalemia present (or 10 g PO daily for ongoing control as needed)
Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV over 5-10 min if severe hyperkalemia with ECG changes (then reassess)
Regular insulin 10 units IV with dextrose 25 g IV if moderate-severe hyperkalemia (then monitor glucose)
Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg PO TID if metabolic acidosis present and patient can take PO (or IV bicarbonate in severe acidosis per protocol)
non_pharmacological: Assess volume status; restrict sodium (e.g., <2 g/day) and fluid if edematous/hyponatremic
Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, iodinated contrast if avoidable, aminoglycosides); review OTC/herbals
Dietary potassium restriction if potassium elevated
Monitor intake/output and daily weights if inpatient or close outpatient monitoring if stable

Nephrology referral/consult for suspected glomerulonephritis/AKI; consider need for additional workup not listed (urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein/creatinine, complements, ANCA/anti-GBM, hepatitis/HIV, renal ultrasound) and possible kidney biopsy based on severity
Escalate to urgent care/ED if oliguria/anuria, rapidly rising creatinine, refractory hyperkalemia, pulmonary edema, or uremic symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat basic metabolic panel and kidney function within 24-72 hours depending on severity (same day/24h if hyperkalemia, acidosis, or rapidly changing creatinine; otherwise within 48-72h) and nephrology follow-up within 1 week (sooner if worsening).

must_include: Kidney inflammation is impairing filtration, reflected by higher creatinine/BUN and lower eGFR
Potential consequences: fluid retention, high blood pressure, electrolyte imbalance (especially potassium), and acid buildup
Mevalonic aciduria is on the differential but is less likely without recurrent fevers/systemic inflammatory episodes; additional history/workup helps distinguish
Immediate priorities: protect the kidneys, manage blood pressure/volume, and correct dangerous potassium or acidosis if present
Medication purpose and key risks (diuretics can dehydrate/worsen kidney function if overused; potassium-lowering therapies require monitoring; insulin therapy can cause hypoglycemia)
Need for repeat labs soon and possible nephrology evaluation and further testing
Review allergies: penicillin (urticarial rash) and shellfish (hives) and clarify reaction severity/timing
Clarify that shellfish allergy does not automatically mean iodine/contrast allergy, but contrast exposure should still be discussed given kidney function and risk mitigation
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: chest pain, shortness of breath, confusion, fainting, very low urine output, severe weakness/palpitations (possible hyperkalemia), rapidly worsening swelling
Avoid NSAIDs and other nephrotoxins; check with clinician before new OTC meds/supplements
Low-sodium diet and fluid guidance based on swelling/sodium level
Potassium restriction if potassium is high (review high-potassium foods) and adherence to follow-up labs

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for nephritis/AKI: onset and trend of urine output, hematuria/foamy urine, edema, dyspnea, flank pain, fever/sore throat/skin infection, rash/arthralgias, recent NSAIDs/antibiotics/contrast, volume losses, comorbid HTN/DM, pregnancy status if relevant, and baseline kidney function.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm reported penicillin and shellfish reactions, severity, timing, and any anaphylaxis features; document clearly.

Initial nephritis/AKI workup and risk stratification: BMP (Cr, BUN, K, CO2/HCO3), CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein/creatinine ratio (or ACR), urine sodium/creatinine if prerenal vs intrinsic unclear, +/- ESR/CRP; consider complement (C3/C4), ASO/anti-DNase B, ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, hepatitis/HIV if indicated by history.
Order kidney ultrasound (or equivalent imaging order) if available in the benchmark to assess obstruction/size; if imaging not represented, document rationale and proceed with lab-based evaluation.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen planned therapies (e.g., ACEi/ARB, diuretics, potassium binders, bicarbonate, insulin/dextrose, antibiotics if needed) against current meds and renal dosing constraints.
Interpret results to confirm nephritic picture (hematuria, proteinuria, RBC casts), quantify kidney dysfunction (eGFR/Cr), and identify emergencies (hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, pulmonary edema).

Make primary diagnosis of nephritis (and specify likely subtype if supported) and document differential including mevalonic aciduria as less likely without recurrent fevers/systemic inflammatory episodes; outline next diagnostic steps.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, consequences, and differential; explain treatment priorities/risks and monitoring; address allergy/contrast concerns and red flags; provide lifestyle guidance (avoid NSAIDs, sodium/potassium guidance, follow-up labs).
Repeat/confirm allergy check immediately before prescribing if new medication class is introduced or if allergy history was incomplete; required critical gating step.

Critical check before ordering any potentially nephrotoxic/contraindicated tests (e.g., iodinated contrast studies) and before prescribing; ensure renal function, pregnancy status if relevant, and hemodynamic status are considered.

Initiate appropriate L1-safe management: supportive care and risk mitigation (e.g., stop nephrotoxins, consider renal-dosed meds; treat hyperkalemia/acidosis/volume overload if present; avoid contraindicated agents).
Arrange repeat labs soon (24–72h depending on severity), blood pressure monitoring, and nephrology referral/urgent evaluation if severe findings; provide clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety-netting.

steps: ASK focused nephritis/AKI history and red-flag symptoms
ORDER_LAB: BMP, CBC, UA with microscopy, urine protein quantification

DIAGNOSE nephritis/AKI; outline differential and note mevalonic aciduria less likely absent recurrent fevers

Thinks glucagonoma is the same as 'regular diabetes' and that controlling sugar alone will make the tumor shrink.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 56-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “My belly has been getting bigger and I feel really bloated, like there’s fluid in there.” Early in the visit, also volunteer: ankle swelling by day’s end, unintentional weight loss despite similar eating, and increased fatigue.

If asked about details, add: tight/uncomfortable abdomen after meals, shortness of breath when lying flat or when very full, frequent urination with excessive thirst/dry mouth, waking at night to pee, intermittent loose stools, and easy bruising.

Do not mention unless directly asked about skin/mouth/eyes: a red sore rash that migrates (groin/buttocks, sometimes near mouth) leaving darker patches, sore smooth tongue and cracked mouth corners, and blurry vision later in the day.

Be resistant/embarrassed about the groin rash; minimize it as “probably yeast/jock itch” unless the clinician normalizes it. If asked about sexual function, reluctantly say libido is down and you’ve had trouble with erections.

Express misconceptions: worry about food intolerance/celiac, or fatty liver causing fluid. Voice cost concerns and try to postpone imaging/specialists. If met with empathy, relax and share more.

medications: Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM monthly
Betaine anhydrous 3 g PO twice daily
Levocarnitine 660 mg PO three times daily
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO daily
confounders: Chronic metabolic acidosis from methylmalonic acidemia (can mimic/worsen cachexia and tachypnea)
Macrocytic anemia related to inborn error of metabolism and nutritional deficiency (may obscure anemia from chronic disease/malnutrition)
Protein-calorie malnutrition/hypoalbuminemia contributing to edema/ascites independent of portal hypertension
Possible hepatic metastases causing cholestatic liver enzyme pattern and portal hypertension-related ascites (can be misattributed to primary liver disease)
Hypercoagulable risk from severe hyperhomocysteinemia (increases risk of portal vein thrombosis as an alternate cause of ascites)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Glucagonoma (pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor secreting glucagon)
acceptable_alternatives: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor with glucagon hypersecretion
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting plasma glucagon (classically >500 pg/mL; often >1000 pg/mL) on an appropriate assay
Hyperglycemia consistent with glucagon excess (elevated fasting serum glucose and/or elevated HbA1c)
Supportive neuroendocrine tumor marker elevation (Chromogranin A elevated) if not confounded (e.g., PPI use, renal dysfunction)

Clinical picture compatible with glucagonoma syndrome and/or complications (e.g., diabetes, weight loss, rash, thromboembolism); in this case, ascites prompting evaluation for liver metastases/portal hypertension or hypoalbuminemia
Exclusion of common alternative causes of ascites (cirrhosis/portal HTN, heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, malignancy) via appropriate workup (imaging/paracentesis) in the full case

expected_lab_results.Plasma glucagon (fasting): Markedly elevated above reference range; typically >500 pg/mL and may be >1000 pg/mL in glucagonoma
expected_lab_results.Chromogranin A: Elevated (supportive of neuroendocrine tumor), recognizing false elevations with PPIs/renal impairment
expected_lab_results.Beta-hydroxybutyrate: Normal or mildly elevated; significant ketosis not typical unless severe insulin deficiency
expected_lab_results.Albumin: Often low if advanced disease/malnutrition or hepatic involvement; low albumin can contribute to ascites
expected_lab_results.Total protein: Low-normal or low if hypoalbuminemia/malnutrition; may be altered with hepatic disease
expected_lab_results.AST: May be elevated if liver metastases or hepatic injury; can be normal if no hepatic involvement

medications: Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks
Octreotide 100-150 mcg SC three times daily (bridge for symptom control until LAR effective, if needed)
Spironolactone 100 mg PO daily (for ascites; titrate as needed)
Furosemide 40 mg PO daily (for ascites; use with spironolactone, titrate to response)
Insulin (basal-bolus regimen; dose individualized) for hyperglycemia if not controlled with non-insulin therapy
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis and stage with dedicated pancreatic imaging and liver imaging; obtain tissue diagnosis when feasible (EUS-guided biopsy or biopsy of metastatic site)
Multidisciplinary referral (endocrinology, oncology, surgical oncology, gastroenterology/hepatology)
Evaluate ascites with diagnostic paracentesis (cell count, albumin/SAAG, total protein, cytology, culture) to determine etiology and guide management
Surgical resection of localized primary tumor when resectable; consider debulking or liver-directed therapies if metastatic and symptomatic
Dietary sodium restriction for ascites; monitor daily weights and volume status
Screen and manage complications: diabetes management, nutritional support, consider VTE risk assessment and prophylaxis per risk/contraindications
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, weight/ascites status, and glycemic control within 1-2 weeks after initiation; repeat relevant labs and treatment response assessment within 4-6 weeks, then every 3 months or per oncology/endocrinology plan

must_include: Glucagonoma is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that produces excess glucagon
Excess glucagon can cause high blood sugar and systemic symptoms; ascites may reflect liver involvement or low albumin and requires evaluation
Diagnosis relies on markedly elevated fasting glucagon plus supportive labs and imaging/biopsy to confirm and stage
Somatostatin analogs (e.g., octreotide) reduce hormone secretion and can improve symptoms and biochemical abnormalities
Definitive management depends on stage: surgery if localized; additional systemic and/or liver-directed therapies if metastatic
Ascites management includes sodium restriction, diuretics, and often paracentesis depending on severity and cause
Diabetes/hyperglycemia management plan and monitoring approach
Discuss expected benefits and common adverse effects of octreotide (GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes) and when to seek care
Explain warning signs requiring urgent evaluation (worsening abdominal distension, fever, severe pain, shortness of breath, confusion, GI bleeding)
Acknowledge penicillin allergy and confirm no penicillin-class antibiotics will be used unless specifically evaluated
Low-sodium diet and daily weight monitoring for ascites
Diabetes self-management basics (home glucose monitoring, hypoglycemia recognition if on insulin)
Importance of follow-up appointments, imaging, and lab monitoring to assess tumor control and complications

Communication milestone bundle: explain suspected syndromic diagnosis and confirmatory testing; discuss key risks (diabetes, dyslipidemia, fatty liver disease, sleep apnea, behavioral challenges) and need for multidisciplinary care; provide lifestyle/hyperphagia safety planning and urgent return precautions.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions with current meds/supplements (especially if starting metformin, GLP-1 RA, or statin).

If indicated by age/comorbidities and after safety checks: initiate evidence-based pharmacotherapy for metabolic risk (e.g., metformin for insulin resistance/prediabetes; consider GLP-1 RA for obesity if appropriate; statin if dyslipidemia meets criteria), with counseling on benefits/risks and monitoring.

Set measurable goals and timeline: weight trajectory, activity targets, lab targets; arrange reassessment (e.g., 4–12 weeks for medication tolerance/weight; 3–6 months labs) and referrals (sleep study, endocrinology, genetics, nutrition, behavioral supports).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting plan, and providing return precautions.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly states that the combination of developmental delay/intellectual disability, obesity/hyperphagia, and dysmorphic features suggests a syndromic etiology and names Prader-Willi syndrome as a leading consideration.
Agent recommends confirmatory genetic testing with DNA methylation analysis of 15q11-q13 and explains what the test is for and what results would mean.

Agent discusses key associated risks requiring screening and longitudinal care: diabetes/prediabetes, dyslipidemia, fatty liver disease, obstructive sleep apnea, and behavioral challenges, and recommends multidisciplinary management.

Agent elicits and documents caregiver goals, barriers, and environment factors (food access/supervision at home/school, resources, behavioral supports) and offers linkage to community/school services.

Agent provides hyperphagia safety planning (supervision, locked food storage, consistent routines across caregivers) and gives clear urgent-care warnings (severe abdominal pain/vomiting, dehydration, hypoglycemia symptoms if on glucose-lowering therapy).

Agent delivers lifestyle counseling with specific, actionable items: scheduled meals/snacks, portion control, high-fiber/lean protein emphasis, avoidance of sugar-sweetened beverages, daily movement plan adapted to developmental level, reduced sedentary time, and sleep hygiene.
Agent includes monitoring guidance: weight trend approach (avoid daily weighing if distressing), follow-up labs, and screening for sleep apnea and fatty liver.
Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents an allergy check and avoids allergens/exposures (amoxicillin-clavulanate and latex).

Before any prescribing, agent performs a contraindication check (e.g., renal function and risk factors for metformin; pregnancy considerations where relevant; pancreatitis history and gallbladder disease considerations for GLP-1 RA; liver disease and myopathy risk for statins).
Agent performs a drug interaction check prior to prescribing when feasible and adjusts plan if interactions are identified (non-critical but expected).

When medications are chosen, agent explains expected benefits, common adverse effects (GI effects with metformin/GLP-1; myalgias and liver enzyme monitoring with statin), and rare but serious risks (e.g., pancreatitis risk with GLP-1) and provides a monitoring plan.
Agent sets measurable goals and a clear reassessment timeline (weight trajectory, activity targets, lab targets) and arranges follow-up/referrals appropriate to suspected Prader-Willi syndrome.

minimal_paths.description: Optimal path: confirm syndromic suspicion, order confirmatory testing and metabolic screening, deliver hyperphagia safety/lifestyle counseling, then consider meds with required safety checks and follow-up.
must_collect: weight trajectory and diet pattern (SSBs, portions, meal timing)
sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness)
allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis)
caregiver priorities and barriers (school/home food access, resources)
steps: ORDER_LAB (DNA methylation 15q11-q13; A1c/fasting glucose; fasting lipids; CMP with AST/ALT; +/- TSH/free T4)
DIAGNOSE (suspected/confirmed Prader-Willi syndrome; document differential if negative)
EDUCATE (diagnosis + risks + multidisciplinary care; hyperphagia safety; lifestyle; urgent precautions)
PRESCRIBE (only if indicated; after contraindication check; counsel adverse effects/monitoring)

minimal_paths.description: Acceptable minimal path when labs/genetics pending or caregiver prefers non-pharmacologic start: focus on diagnosis explanation, safety planning, and structured lifestyle; schedule close follow-up for results and next steps.
steps: DIAGNOSE (syndromic cause suspected; PWS leading consideration pending testing)
EDUCATE (diagnosis, risks, lifestyle, safety/urgent precautions, resources)

acceptable_variants.description: Medication-first variant when significant metabolic disease is already documented (e.g., known prediabetes/diabetes or severe dyslipidemia): initiate pharmacotherapy after safety checks while genetic confirmation is pending.
steps: ORDER_LAB (confirmatory genetics + baseline safety labs relevant to chosen medication)
PRESCRIBE (after contraindication check; counsel benefits/risks and monitoring)
EDUCATE (diagnosis suspicion + risks + lifestyle + hyperphagia safety + urgent precautions)

acceptable_variants.description: Expanded diagnostic variant if Prader-Willi methylation testing is negative: broaden differential and genetic evaluation while continuing metabolic management and safety planning.
steps: ORDER_LAB (PWS DNA methylation; metabolic panel; consider chromosomal microarray/targeted gene testing if methylation negative)
DIAGNOSE (alternative syndromic obesity/DD pathway if PWS excluded)
